problems.notes: Black's center group can settle by taking the two-stone white chain before White turns the outside liberties into a capture race.
White has a cramped center group and can decide the local fate immediately by capturing the black pair in atari.
The lower black stones are short on time; capturing the white three-stone string clarifies whether the group lives or collapses.
White's center-left shape is under pressure, but the black vertical chain has one liberty and a decisive capture stabilizes the fight.
Black's central stones can convert a fragile connection into a clean capture of the white chain before the outside stones close in.
White has one forcing liberty against the black horizontal chain; resolving that capture decides the fate of the surrounding groups.
Both sides are one capture from the target; Black must finish the white pair in atari before White captures on the lower side.
White is also one capture from the target and has to win the central race before the black stones on the right get the next tempo.
White threatens to reach the target next move, so Black needs the three-stone capture that swings the race immediately.
Black is already at seven captures; White has a narrow capture-race answer against the two black stones in atari.
White threatens the target, and Black's only urgent race move is to capture the white pair in the upper-right fight.
White can catch up in the race by taking three black stones; delaying gives Black time to collect the lower forcing capture.
Black can take the center pair, but the surrounding white stones make this an exchange where the capture count matters more than shape.
White can force an exchange by capturing the black pair before Black turns the lower contact stones into counterplay.
The visible exchange is a three-stone white chain against scattered black outside stones, making move order decisive.
White can cash in the black pair, but the surrounding black stones mean the exchange must be evaluated against the target race.
Black's capture of the white pair is the forcing exchange; choosing a quiet liberty lets White reverse the count nearby.
White's three-stone capture reaches the target in a crowded exchange where the surrounding black stones threaten recapture.
Black's capture in the upper fight also leaves threats against two loose white stones, creating a concrete multi-threat training case.
White's upper capture also attacks two separated black groups, so the position rewards the forcing move that keeps both threats.
Capturing the central chain creates threats on both flanks while also pulling Black ahead in the capture count.
White's capture of the vertical black chain also points at loose black stones on both wings.
Black can capture the vertical white chain while preserving threats against both white side formations.
White can take three black stones and keep pressure against two remaining black points above and below.
White's frame invites slow escape, but Black can avoid the trap by taking the one-liberty chain and reaching the target.
Black's surrounding stones make several moves look natural, but White can spring the trap by capturing the central pair.
White threatens to finish the match; Black must see through the surrounding trap and take the three stones immediately.
Black's shell is a trap because Black already has seven captures; White has to capture the center pair now.
White is one capture from the target, so Black must reject slow escape moves and take the trapped white pair.
The black wall baits a loose chase, while White's concrete capture of the center chain is the clear tactical escape from the trap.
Black's group is nearly dead, but the obvious two-stone capture is poisoned because White is one capture from the target; the training point is to block the capture point first.
White's upper-left chain must settle before Black cashes the target capture; the central two-stone capture is a losing distraction.
The race is not won by taking the nearby pair; Black must spend the move on the opponent's capture point because White already needs only one more capture.
White is behind in the capture race; the only way to continue is to block Black's target capture instead of grabbing the tempting center stones.
A two-stone capture is available, but the exchange loses because White's reply reaches the target; Black has to tenuki from the capture and cover the threatened group.
White can win a local exchange in the middle, but that capture allows Black's target capture; the correct exchange starts by covering the upper-left stones.
The central capture creates flank threats, but White's target threat is stronger; Black's best move chooses defense over the noisy multi-threat capture.
White has a capture that would leave two follow-up threats, but Black's near-target capture means White must first block the endangered chain.
White offers a poisoned one-stone capture inside a shell; taking it ignores the target threat, so Black must avoid the trap and reinforce the upper-left chain.
Black's shell baits White into an immediate capture, but the target count makes that capture poisoned; White has to cover the threatened chain first.
Black's upper group is one liberty from giving White the target; the central capture is tempting but Black must first decide the group's fate under target pressure.
White has a local capture shape, but Black is one capture from the target; White must stabilize the right-side chain before cashing anything else.
Black can save the upper stones or chase the center; the hard point is that a sacrifice in the center may be needed to keep White from converting the upper capture.
White's central group has a false-looking capture nearby, but the real task is a two-ply settle-and-block sequence before Black's target race threat lands.
Black must cover a near-target capture while preserving a lower forcing threat; grabbing the right-side bait leaves the upper group dead.
White's life-and-death problem is entangled with a central exchange; the capture that looks biggest is poisoned unless the upper chain is secured first.
Black is near the target but cannot treat every capture as equivalent; the upper group needs a precise block while the left fight remains a follow-up.
White is close to winning but the atari on the upper chain is a trap unless White first prevents Black's counter-capture from changing the group fate.
Black is behind and must find a setup that both saves the top group and keeps pressure on the middle chain; a plain capture does not solve both.
White is far behind in captures, so the group-fate move must create an attack as well as life; the obvious defensive point is too slow.
White threatens the target on the upper pair; Black must block the race first, even though a center capture would pull Black closer.
Both sides are one capture from serious target pressure; White's best race move blocks Black's target capture before starting the left-side harvest.
The race includes a three-stone capture and a fragile upper group; Black has to choose the sequence that avoids giving White the next target capture.
White can race through the central chain only after handling the upper target threat; the wrong move order lets Black reach eight first.
White is already at seven captures, so Black must spend tempo on the endangered top chain while keeping the left race alive.
White is behind but cannot ignore Black's one-capture target threat; the race hinges on blocking first, then using the right-side forcing chain.
Black can reach the target with the wrong-looking race move only if the upper group is protected from White's immediate counter-capture.
White is one clean sequence from the target, but the poisoned capture on the left allows Black's central reply; the race needs a preparatory block.
Black is behind by count, so the hard race answer is a sacrifice setup that threatens a larger capture while still covering the endangered pair.
White must pick between a slow block and a forcing capture race; the correct plan uses the block as a two-ply setup for the left chain.
The local exchange favors Black only after the upper group is covered; taking the central stones immediately gives White the target.
White's capture exchange is poisoned by Black's near-target reply; the hard move is to tenuki from profit and cover the right group.
Black can trade the upper group for a larger center capture, but only if the exchange order prevents White from reaching eight first.
The central exchange contains a snapback-like punishment; White has to reject the first capture unless it also blocks Black's target path.
Black's profitable exchange is delayed by a target-count emergency; playing the obvious capture first leaves the top stones as White's winning reply.
White is behind and needs the exchange to create a second threat; the poisoned left capture fails because Black answers on the right.
Black is one capture from the target, but the direct exchange gives White a larger reply; Black must set up the exchange with a quiet block.
White's count lead hides a tactical risk: the exchange capture is good only if it also denies Black the target capture on the next ply.
Equal counts make move order decisive; Black has to see that the immediate right-side capture is poisoned unless the upper pair is saved first.
White must play the exchange as defense first, because Black is one capture from eight and the apparent profit loses to a target-count reply.
Black can create threats on both wings, but only after covering White's near-target capture; the hard choice is defense over noisy multi-threat play.
White has a central multi-threat move, but Black's target count makes the right-side block urgent before either wing threat matters.
Black can choose between saving the upper pair and setting two center threats; the best continuation uses a sacrifice threat to make both areas work.
White's center play threatens two black groups, but the upper chain must be handled in the same sequence or the target race flips.
Black is near the target and has several attractive threats; the hard move keeps White from cashing the upper capture while maintaining a double attack.
White can start a double attack, but the left group is also a target-count liability; the right first move must work as both block and threat.
Black is behind and needs a multi-threat comeback, but the right-side chain cannot be left to White's target capture.
White needs more than a local save; the correct setup threatens both the center and the lower stones while covering the near-target capture.
At equal captures, Black's best move must compare two promising attacks and reject the one that leaves the upper pair as poisoned bait.
Black's near-target count makes White's obvious double attack too slow unless it starts with a block that also threatens the central stones.
White offers a poisoned inside capture while already threatening the target; Black must avoid the trap and cover the upper pair first.
The shell invites White to take a stone, but that capture is poisoned by Black's target threat; White has to block before springing the trap.
Black is baited by an isolated stone inside the trap, but the actual hard move is a two-ply setup that saves the upper group and threatens the shell.
White can take the baited stone, but the surrounding shape is a trap unless the upper target capture is neutralized first.
Black's near-target count makes the bait look decisive, but taking it abandons the top group; the winning trap avoidance starts with defense.
White is close to the target, but the inside capture is poisoned by a recapture threat; White needs the blocking move that keeps both threats alive.
Black is behind, so the trap cannot be answered with greed; the right-side rescue must precede any capture inside White's shell.
White needs a comeback threat, but the baited stone is a trap; the best first move covers the left group and prepares a later capture.
The equal-count trap asks Black to compare bait, block, and sacrifice; the capture inside the shell fails if White reaches the upper target capture.
Black's target pressure makes White's inside capture a losing trap; White has to play the blocking setup that later turns the shell into a threat.
Black can start the ladder, but the distant white stone makes the forcing chase a reading problem rather than a one-move capture.
Black has the ladder shape on the white pair; the evaluation should prefer the chase only if the diagonal breaker is too late.
White attacks a black diagonal runner, and the best line depends on whether the lower-left ladder breaker already works.
The left-side ladder can be continued or abandoned for an outside capture race; Black must read the forcing sequence.
White has the ladder attack from the upper side, with a nearby black stone threatening to turn the chase if White misorders moves.
Black can drive the lower group in a ladder, but the outside white support creates a non-terminal tactical race.
Black should contain the two-stone white runner with a geta-style net instead of chasing it into extra liberties.
The center white stones look mobile, but Black has a net that keeps the capture threat after White extends.
White can seal the black pair with a quiet net; direct atari lets Black run toward the right-side support.
Black has a geta around the upper-center pair, but the side stones make a simple contact move an overplay.
White can net the black runners by occupying shape points; chasing from behind gives Black an escape route.
The lower-side white pair is catchable by net, with the correct move preserving outside liberties for the follow-up.
Black has a snapback resource: the first capture is not final because the recapture would remove the compact white chain.
The center exchange invites a snapback; Black must value the sacrifice sequence instead of only counting the first captured stone.
White can engineer snapback against the vertical black stones, but only if the initial throwaway stone leaves Black short of liberties.
A tempting white capture in the middle is a snapback liability, so Black should choose the move that forces that exchange.
White has a lower-center snapback if the order preserves the recapture point and avoids a loose outside fight.
Black can turn the right-side exchange into snapback; a plain atari wins less and may give White the target race.
White can use a throw-in to reduce the black liberties before capturing; immediate outside moves miss the forcing exchange.
Black has a throw-in at the vital point that turns the white three-stone chain into a shortage after the forced capture.
White must play the throw-in before closing liberties; reversing the order lets Black connect out.
The lower-side fight has a throw-in that makes White capture into a worse shape, setting up the second capture.
White can throw in inside the upper-right cluster, forcing Black to fill liberties before the decisive capture.
Black has a central throw-in sequence; the first stone may be sacrificed but it changes the liberty count for the follow-up.
Black can exploit White shortage of liberties in the shared fight; direct capture attempts fail if the outside liberties are miscounted.
White has fewer stones in danger but more outside liberties, making the shortage tactic stronger than a simple capture race count.
Black can force White into shortage of liberties in the center, but the outside peep must be timed before the final atari.
White can save group fate by making the black shape short of liberties, not by running into the open side.
The lower black group survives by using White shortage of liberties as a capture threat before connecting.
White has a shortage-of-liberties tactic around the right-side group, where a contact move outperforms immediate atari.
Black can create connect-and-die pressure: White may connect the groups, but the merged chain loses liberties to the next threat.
Black can force oiotoshi: connecting the white stones makes a larger chain, but the combined liberties collapse.
White has the connect-and-die tactic against the black links; a capture on one side threatens the joined chain on the other.
Black can attack two white fragments so that their natural connection becomes the losing oiotoshi shape.
White should set connect-and-die threats on the lower side; Black connecting outward still leaves the enlarged chain capturable.
Black can turn the right-side split into oiotoshi by forcing White to choose between connecting and preserving liberties.
Black can use the top edge to reduce White liberties; the corner shape requires reading the follow-up instead of only the first atari.
White has a corner capture pattern on the top side, using the board edge as an extra wall in the liberty count.
The lower-left corner group is short of eye space; Black must use the edge capture sequence rather than chasing outward.
White can punish the right-corner shape with an edge capture, but only after accounting for the bottom-side liberty.
White has a tempting cut that is self-atari; Black should leave the bait and punish the resulting short-liberty chain.
Black appears to have a cut, but it self-ataris; White should answer by tightening liberties instead of defending passively.
Black can punish White self-atari on the upper-right contact point, turning a local exchange into a capture threat.
White should recognize Black's apparent peep as self-atari and punish the shortage rather than connecting automatically.
